questions.q: Explain the three service lifetimes: Singleton, Scoped, and Transient.

answer.oneLiner: Singleton means one instance for the whole app lifetime. Scoped means one per HTTP request. Transient means a brand-new instance every time one is requested.
answer.detail: When you call builder.Services.AddSingleton, the container creates the object once and hands out the same reference to every caller forever. AddScoped creates one object when the HTTP request starts and disposes it when the request ends - all code within that request shares the same instance. AddTransient creates a fresh object every single time the container resolves the type, regardless of how many times that happens in one request. In a web app the scope boundary is the HTTP request, which is why DbContext is scoped by default - one unit of work per request.
keyPoints: Singleton is created once at startup and shared across all requests - it must be thread-safe.
Scoped lives and dies with one HTTP request - the default for DbContext and repository classes.
Transient is created fresh on every injection - best for cheap, stateless helpers like formatters or validators.
Never register a DbContext as Singleton - it is not thread-safe and will corrupt change tracking.
Getting the lifetime wrong is the most common DI bug - a Singleton holding request-specific data leaks data across users.

code.note: Each service is registered with the lifetime that matches how it is used - shared global state, per-request unit of work, or throw-away helper.

answer.analogy: Singleton is the office printer; Scoped is your desk for the day; Transient is a sticky note you use and throw away.
redFlags: Saying 'Scoped means per class' - it means per HTTP request, not per class.
Registering DbContext as Singleton - guaranteed concurrency bugs under load.
Not knowing that a Singleton leaks data across users when it holds request-specific state.
Confusing Transient with Scoped - Transient creates a new object every injection, even within the same request.

followUpsQA.q: Why is DbContext registered as Scoped and not Transient?
followUpsQA.a: DbContext tracks entity changes within a unit of work. If each repository gets its own Transient DbContext, they cannot coordinate - changes tracked in one context are invisible to the other, breaking the unit-of-work pattern.

followUpsQA.q: What must a Singleton service always be?
followUpsQA.a: Thread-safe, because it is shared across all concurrent requests. If it holds mutable state without locking, you will get race conditions.

followUpsQA.q: What is the captive dependency problem?
followUpsQA.a: When a Singleton depends on a Scoped service, the Scoped service gets captured inside the Singleton and lives as long as the app, not just one request - effectively becoming a Singleton with stale data.

followUpsQA.q: When would you actually use Transient?
followUpsQA.a: For lightweight, stateless helpers that are cheap to create - things like an email template formatter, a CSV builder, or an input sanitizer.

followUpsQA.q: Does ASP.NET Core detect mismatched lifetimes?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - in Development with scope validation enabled, the container throws an InvalidOperationException at startup if a Singleton tries to depend on a Scoped service.

dialogue.text: Can you walk me through the three service lifetimes in ASP.NET Core DI?

Sure. Singleton is one instance shared across the entire app lifetime. Scoped is one instance per HTTP request. Transient is a brand-new instance every time it is resolved from the container. The most important one to get right is Scoped - its boundary is the HTTP request.
Why do you say Scoped is the most important to get right?

Because DbContext is Scoped by default and for good reason - one unit of work per request. If you accidentally register it as Singleton, the same DbContext gets shared across concurrent requests. DbContext is not thread-safe, so you get corrupted change tracking and race conditions under load.
What about Singleton - when is it safe to use?

When the service is either stateless or thread-safe. A good example is an exchange rate converter that caches rates in a ConcurrentDictionary - it is safe to share. But if a Singleton holds per-request data like the current user ID, that data bleeds across users.
You mentioned Scoped boundary is the HTTP request - what if you are in a background service, not handling an HTTP request?

Background services are Singletons. To safely use a Scoped service like DbContext inside one, I inject IServiceScopeFactory and call CreateScope() inside a using block, then resolve the DbContext from that scope. When the using block ends the scope and DbContext are disposed properly.
Does the container help you catch lifetime mismatches, or do you have to find them yourself?

In Development the container has scope validation enabled by default. If a Singleton tries to depend on a Scoped service, it throws an InvalidOperationException at startup when the service is first resolved - which catches the bug before it reaches production.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

answer.oneLiner: DI hands object creation to a container so classes depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations - giving you loose coupling, easy testability, and centralized lifetime management.
answer.detail: Without DI, a class calls new PaymentGateway() inside its own body. That hard-wires it to one specific implementation - you cannot swap it for a test double or a different provider without editing the class. With DI, the class declares what it needs as a constructor parameter - for example, IPaymentGateway - and the container figures out which concrete type to inject at runtime. This separation follows the Dependency Inversion Principle (the D in SOLID): high-level modules should not depend on low-level details. The container also manages when objects are created and disposed, removing that burden from your code.
keyPoints: Classes depend on interfaces, not concrete types - so you can swap implementations without changing consumer code.
Unit testing becomes trivial - just pass a mock or stub into the constructor instead of the real dependency.
The container manages object creation, lifetime, and disposal - your classes do not call 'new' for their dependencies.
DI is the Dependency Inversion Principle in practice - the D in SOLID.
Centralising service registration in Program.cs means one place to see and change what implementation backs each interface.

code.note: The GOOD version lets you inject a fake IEmailSender in tests and swap the real SMTP sender for SendGrid in production without touching OrderService.

answer.analogy: Don't build your own engine - request one and let the container decide which model fits.
redFlags: Saying DI is just about avoiding 'new' - it is about depending on abstractions and inverting control.
Not knowing what Dependency Inversion Principle means - DI is its practical implementation.
Confusing DI with a service locator - calling GetService inside business logic is the anti-pattern DI is meant to replace.
Thinking DI is only useful for testing - it also enables swapping implementations and centralising lifetime management.

followUpsQA.q: What SOLID principle does DI implement?
followUpsQA.a: The Dependency Inversion Principle - high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not on low-level concrete implementations.

followUpsQA.q: How does DI make unit testing easier?
followUpsQA.a: Because dependencies are injected via the constructor, you can pass in a mock or stub during tests without needing the real database, email server, or payment gateway.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between DI and a service locator?
followUpsQA.a: With DI the container pushes dependencies into the class. With a service locator the class pulls them out by calling the container directly - this hides dependencies and makes code harder to test.

followUpsQA.q: Who manages the lifetime and disposal of services?
followUpsQA.a: The DI container - it creates, caches, and disposes services according to the registered lifetime, so your classes do not need to manage that.

dialogue.text: Why do we use dependency injection? Isn't it just extra complexity?

It looks like extra ceremony at first, but the payoff is real. Without it, classes call 'new ConcreteService()' inside their own bodies - hard-wiring themselves to one implementation. With DI they declare IPaymentGateway in the constructor and the container decides what to inject. You can swap implementations without touching the consumer.
How does that actually help in day-to-day development?

Testing is the biggest win. For a unit test on OrderService I just pass in a mock IEmailSender - no real SMTP server needed. In production the container injects SendGrid. If we switch providers, I register a new implementation in Program.cs and nothing else changes.
What principle is DI embodying here?

The Dependency Inversion Principle - the D in SOLID. High-level modules like OrderService should not depend on low-level details like SmtpEmailSender. Both should depend on the IEmailSender abstraction. DI is what makes that practical at runtime.
Could I just use a service locator and call GetService anywhere I need something?

You could, but that is the service locator anti-pattern. The class dependencies become invisible because they are not declared in the constructor. You cannot tell what a class needs just by looking at its signature, and testing becomes painful because you have to configure a full container for every test.
Who actually manages the disposal of services - the developer or the container?

The container. If a Scoped service implements IDisposable, the container calls Dispose at the end of the request scope. For Singletons it disposes on app shutdown. Your classes just ask for what they need and the container handles creation and cleanup.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the captive dependency problem (Scoped vs Singleton)?

answer.oneLiner: A captive dependency happens when a Singleton holds a reference to a Scoped service - the short-lived service is kept alive as long as the Singleton, causing stale data and thread-safety bugs across all requests.
answer.detail: When a Singleton is built, the container injects its constructor dependencies once and stores them in its fields forever. If one of those dependencies is a Scoped service - like a DbContext - that instance is captured inside the Singleton and never replaced per request. From that moment on, every request in the entire application shares the same DbContext. DbContext is not thread-safe, so concurrent requests corrupt each other's change tracking. The container detects some of these mismatches in Development via scope validation and throws at startup, but it does not catch every case - for example when the Scoped service arrives through a factory wrapper. The fix is to not inject the Scoped service directly into the Singleton. Instead inject IServiceScopeFactory and create a fresh manual scope each time you need to do the work.
keyPoints: A Singleton that injects a Scoped service captures it forever - the Scoped service never gets disposed and refreshed per request.
A captured DbContext is shared across all concurrent requests, which breaks thread safety and corrupts change tracking.
In Development the container throws InvalidOperationException at startup when it detects a direct Singleton-to-Scoped dependency.
The fix is to inject IServiceScopeFactory into the Singleton and create a manual scope each time you need the Scoped service.
Background services implementing IHostedService are the most common place this bites developers because they are Singletons.

code.note: CreateScope creates a fresh DI scope each time, giving a clean Scoped DbContext that is disposed when the using block ends.

answer.analogy: Handing a Singleton a Scoped service is like giving a permanent office key to a one-day contractor.
redFlags: Not knowing this problem exists - it causes hard-to-reproduce bugs under concurrent load.
Thinking the container always catches it - scope validation misses indirect cases like factory wrappers.
Saying 'just make the DbContext a Singleton to solve the warning' - that fixes the build error but causes far worse concurrency bugs.
Not knowing that IServiceScopeFactory is the correct fix - it is the built-in solution for this exact scenario.

followUpsQA.q: Does ASP.NET Core always catch captive dependencies?
followUpsQA.a: No - scope validation in Development catches direct Singleton-to-Scoped injection but misses indirect cases where a Scoped service is resolved through a factory or lazy wrapper.

followUpsQA.q: What is the correct fix when a Singleton genuinely needs database access?
followUpsQA.a: Inject IServiceScopeFactory, call CreateScope() inside a using block each time you need to do database work, resolve DbContext from that scope's ServiceProvider, and let the using block dispose the scope when done.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a background service particularly prone to this problem?
followUpsQA.a: IHostedService implementations are registered as Singletons. Developers often inject DbContext directly into them without thinking about the lifetime mismatch, and it looks fine in tests until concurrent load reveals the corruption.

followUpsQA.q: What runtime symptoms appear from a captive DbContext?
followUpsQA.a: InvalidOperationException about a second operation being started on a context still in use, stale query results, or entity tracking conflicts when two concurrent requests touch the same captured context.

dialogue.text: Can you explain the captive dependency problem?

A captive dependency is when a Singleton holds a Scoped service in its constructor. The Singleton lives for the whole app lifetime, so it captures and retains that Scoped instance forever - it never gets disposed and replaced per request the way it should.
What goes wrong when a Singleton captures a DbContext?

That one DbContext is shared across every concurrent request in the app. DbContext is not thread-safe, so two requests writing to it simultaneously corrupt each other's change tracking. You get exceptions like 'A second operation was started on this context before a previous operation completed'.
Does the DI container warn you about this?

In Development with scope validation on, it throws InvalidOperationException at startup when it resolves the Singleton and sees the Scoped dependency directly. But it does not catch every case - if the Scoped service arrives through a factory or lazy wrapper, the check is bypassed.
How do you fix it when a Singleton genuinely needs database access?

Inject IServiceScopeFactory instead of the DbContext. Each time I need to do database work I call _scopeFactory.CreateScope() inside a using block, then resolve InvoiceDbContext from that scope. When the using block ends the scope and the DbContext are disposed properly.
Where does this problem come up most often in real projects?

Background services - anything implementing IHostedService or BackgroundService is registered as a Singleton. Developers inject DbContext directly into them without thinking about the lifetime mismatch, and everything looks fine in dev until concurrent load hits in production and corruption exceptions start appearing.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you register and resolve services in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: Register services in builder.Services using Add{Lifetime}<IInterface, Implementation>() before Build(). Resolve them via constructor injection in your classes, or explicitly with GetRequiredService<T>() when no constructor is available.
answer.detail: Registration happens in Program.cs before builder.Build() is called. You call methods like AddScoped, AddSingleton, or AddTransient on the IServiceCollection, mapping an interface to a concrete type. After Build() the service collection is locked - no new registrations can be added. Resolution is the act of asking the container for an instance. The idiomatic way is constructor injection: declare the interface as a constructor parameter and the framework resolves and injects it automatically. You can also resolve explicitly using IServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<T>() but this should be limited to infrastructure code like factories or Program.cs setup - never scattered through business logic, which would be the service locator anti-pattern.
keyPoints: Register before builder.Build() - the service collection is locked after that point.
Constructor injection is the standard way to resolve - declare the interface as a constructor parameter.
GetRequiredService<T>() throws if the service is not registered; GetService<T>() returns null instead.
Prefer GetRequiredService over GetService - fail fast at startup rather than get a null reference exception later.
Calling GetService inside business logic is the service locator anti-pattern - it hides dependencies and hurts testability.

code.note: Constructor injection makes IShipmentRepo visible and mockable; the service locator version hides it and requires a container in every unit test.

answer.analogy: Declare what you need at the door; don't go hunting for tools inside the room.
redFlags: Injecting IServiceProvider into controllers or services and calling GetService inside methods - that is the service locator anti-pattern.
Not knowing that GetService returns null instead of throwing - unexpected null references are harder to debug than startup exceptions.
Trying to register services after builder.Build() - the container is sealed at that point.
Confusing registration with resolution - registration is the startup setup step, resolution is the per-request runtime step.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between GetRequiredService and GetService?
followUpsQA.a: GetRequiredService throws InvalidOperationException if the service is not registered. GetService returns null. Prefer GetRequiredService so misconfiguration fails loudly at first resolution rather than causing a null reference exception deep in business logic.

followUpsQA.q: Can you register services after builder.Build()?
followUpsQA.a: No - calling builder.Build() locks the IServiceCollection and creates the IServiceProvider. Any attempt to add registrations after that point is ignored or throws.

followUpsQA.q: When is it acceptable to call GetRequiredService manually?
followUpsQA.a: In infrastructure setup code in Program.cs, in factory functions registered via the lambda overload, or when writing framework extension methods. It should never appear inside controller or domain logic.

followUpsQA.q: How do you register a service that requires constructor arguments not available in DI?
followUpsQA.a: Use the factory lambda overload: builder.Services.AddScoped<IPaymentGateway>(sp => new StripeGateway(sp.GetRequiredService<IOptions<StripeSettings>>().Value)).

dialogue.text: Walk me through how you register and resolve services in ASP.NET Core.

Registration goes in Program.cs before builder.Build(). I call AddScoped, AddSingleton, or AddTransient on builder.Services, mapping an interface to a concrete type. Once Build() is called the collection is locked. Resolution is usually constructor injection - declare the interface as a parameter and the framework injects it.
What if you need to resolve something in Program.cs itself, outside a class constructor?

I call app.Services.GetRequiredService<T>() on the WebApplication after Build(). I use GetRequiredService rather than GetService because it throws immediately if the service is missing, surfacing the misconfiguration at startup rather than hiding it as a null reference exception later.
What if a developer injects IServiceProvider into their controller and calls GetService in every action?

That is the service locator anti-pattern. The controller's actual dependencies become invisible - you cannot see from the constructor what it needs, which makes testing painful because you have to set up a full DI container for every unit test.
How do you handle registering a service that requires constructor arguments not available in DI?

I use the factory lambda overload: AddScoped<IPaymentGateway>(sp => new StripeGateway(sp.GetRequiredService<IOptions<StripeSettings>>().Value)). The lambda receives the IServiceProvider so I can resolve other registered services and mix in configuration values.
Can you register the same interface to multiple implementations?

Yes - you can call AddScoped for the same interface multiple times. When you inject IEnumerable<INotificationChannel>, the container hands you all of them in registration order. From .NET 8 you can also use keyed services to resolve a specific implementation by name instead.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is constructor injection and why is it preferred?

answer.oneLiner: Constructor injection means declaring dependencies as constructor parameters. The class cannot be built without them, making all dependencies explicit, required, and immutable from the moment the object is created.
answer.detail: When a class lists its dependencies in the constructor, anyone reading the code immediately knows what that class needs to function. The DI container reads those parameters and injects the registered instances automatically. Because the dependencies are assigned to readonly fields in the constructor, they cannot be changed after construction - the object is always in a valid, complete state. Compare this with property injection, where dependencies are set after construction: the object can exist in a partially initialised state, which is a source of null reference bugs. Method injection exists too but is uncommon - it means passing a dependency into a specific method rather than the whole object's lifetime, which is occasionally useful for per-call concerns. Constructor injection also acts as a natural warning: if a constructor has eight or more parameters it is a signal the class is doing too much and should be split.
keyPoints: Dependencies are declared at the constructor, making them visible to any caller and to the DI container.
The object cannot be in an invalid state - all required dependencies are set before any method can be called.
Dependencies assigned to readonly fields cannot be swapped out after construction, preventing hidden state changes.
A constructor with many parameters is a code smell signalling an SRP violation - split the class.
Property injection is only acceptable for genuinely optional dependencies - it hides required ones and allows incomplete objects.

code.note: The readonly field guarantees IEmailSender is always present and never replaced, eliminating null reference and threading bugs caused by late or missing assignment.

answer.analogy: A plane that needs two engines should list them in the pre-flight checklist, not discover one is missing mid-flight.
redFlags: Saying property injection is fine for required dependencies - it hides them and allows incomplete objects.
Not flagging a constructor with six or more parameters as a design problem - it signals an SRP violation.
Confusing constructor injection with the service locator pattern - the container pushes dependencies in, not the class pulling from the container.
Saying 'readonly is optional on injected fields' - without readonly another method can null out the field after construction.

followUpsQA.q: Why assign constructor dependencies to readonly fields?
followUpsQA.a: Readonly prevents any method from reassigning the field after construction, so the dependency cannot become null or change mid-lifetime - the object is always in a consistent, valid state.

followUpsQA.q: What does a constructor with eight parameters tell you?
followUpsQA.a: The class has too many responsibilities and likely violates the Single Responsibility Principle. The correct fix is to split it into smaller focused classes, not to reduce the parameter count by hiding dependencies.

followUpsQA.q: Is property injection ever acceptable?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, for genuinely optional dependencies where the class works without them and falls back to a default behaviour. It is rare in application code but common in framework extensibility points.

followUpsQA.q: How does constructor injection make unit testing easier?
followUpsQA.a: You instantiate the class by passing mock implementations directly into the constructor - no container setup needed, just 'new InvoiceService(mockEmailSender)' in the test.

dialogue.text: What is constructor injection and why do we prefer it over other styles?

Constructor injection means declaring every dependency as a constructor parameter. The class cannot be instantiated without them, which guarantees it is always in a valid, fully initialised state. The framework reads those parameters and injects the registered services automatically.
What is wrong with property injection for required dependencies?

The object can be constructed and then used before the property is set - that is a NullReferenceException waiting to happen. Dependencies also become invisible: you cannot tell from the constructor signature what the class needs, which makes testing harder and the contract unclear.
Why do you always assign injected dependencies to readonly fields?

Readonly means no method can reassign the field after construction. Without it, some other method could accidentally null out the dependency or replace it with a different instance mid-lifetime. With readonly the object is always in a consistent state from the moment it is created.
I see a class here with nine constructor parameters. What is your reaction?

That is a strong signal of an SRP violation - the class is doing too many things. Nine dependencies means nine responsibilities mixed together. The right fix is to split it into smaller focused classes, not to collapse the parameters into an aggregate wrapper just to shrink the list.
Is there any scenario where you would use method injection instead of constructor injection?

When a dependency is only needed for one specific method and varies per call rather than for the whole object lifetime. ASP.NET Core uses this pattern in middleware, where InvokeAsync parameters are resolved per request from the current scope rather than at construction time.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

answer.oneLiner: Keyed services let you register multiple implementations of the same interface under different string keys and resolve a specific one by name using the [FromKeyedServices] attribute in the constructor.
answer.detail: Before .NET 8, having two implementations of INotificationChannel - say EmailChannel and SmsChannel - and needing to inject one specific implementation into a class required awkward workarounds. You could inject IEnumerable<INotificationChannel> and filter by concrete type, or write a factory class that picked the right one. Both approaches leak implementation details into the consumer. .NET 8 added first-class keyed service support directly in the built-in container. You register each implementation with a key using AddKeyedScoped, AddKeyedSingleton, or AddKeyedTransient, passing a string key as the first argument. To consume a specific implementation you add [FromKeyedServices("key")] to the constructor parameter and the container resolves exactly the implementation registered under that key.
keyPoints: Introduced in .NET 8 - lets you disambiguate multiple implementations of the same interface by a string key.
Register with AddKeyedScoped, AddKeyedSingleton, or AddKeyedTransient and pass the key string as the first argument.
Consume with the [FromKeyedServices("key")] attribute on the constructor parameter.
Replaces the awkward IEnumerable<T> plus OfType<ConcreteClass>() workaround used before .NET 8.
All three lifetimes are supported - Singleton, Scoped, and Transient - and the same captive dependency rules apply.

code.note: The [FromKeyedServices] attribute instructs the container to inject the specific implementation registered under the 'sms' key, keeping the consumer abstraction-only.

answer.analogy: Keyed services are like labeled filing cabinets - you pull exactly from the drawer you need.
redFlags: Not knowing this feature exists in .NET 8 and still proposing IEnumerable<T> workarounds for all multi-implementation scenarios.
Confusing keyed services with named options (IOptions<T>) - they solve completely different problems.
Saying you would write a factory class to solve this - keyed services are simpler and built into the framework since .NET 8.
Assuming keyed services only support Singleton lifetime - all three lifetimes are available.

followUpsQA.q: What problem did keyed services solve?
followUpsQA.a: The awkward workaround of injecting IEnumerable<T> and filtering by concrete type at runtime when you need one specific implementation of an interface from the DI container.

followUpsQA.q: How do you consume a keyed service in a constructor?
followUpsQA.a: Add the [FromKeyedServices("key")] attribute to the constructor parameter - for example: public OrderService([FromKeyedServices("stripe")] IPaymentGateway gateway).

followUpsQA.q: Do keyed services support Scoped lifetime?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - all three lifetimes are available: AddKeyedSingleton, AddKeyedScoped, and AddKeyedTransient.

followUpsQA.q: Can you resolve a keyed service from IServiceProvider directly without a constructor?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - use serviceProvider.GetRequiredKeyedService<INotificationChannel>("sms") when constructor injection is not available, such as in factory code or Program.cs setup.

dialogue.text: What are keyed services introduced in .NET 8?

Keyed services let you register multiple implementations of the same interface under different string keys and then resolve a specific one by name. Before .NET 8 there was no clean built-in way to do this - you had to inject IEnumerable<T> and filter by concrete type, or write a factory class.
Show me how you would register and consume them.

Register like this: AddKeyedScoped<INotificationChannel, EmailChannel>("email") and AddKeyedScoped<INotificationChannel, SmsChannel>("sms"). Then in the constructor I write [FromKeyedServices("sms")] INotificationChannel sms and the container injects exactly the SmsChannel registered under that key.
What was wrong with the IEnumerable<T> workaround used before?

It leaks concrete types into the consumer. Calling .OfType<SmsChannel>().First() means the class now depends on the concrete SmsChannel class, not just the interface - which defeats the purpose of the abstraction. Keyed services keep the consumer working only with INotificationChannel.
What lifetimes do keyed services support?

All three - AddKeyedSingleton, AddKeyedScoped, and AddKeyedTransient. The same captive dependency rules apply here too. A keyed Singleton capturing a keyed Scoped service is still a problem and will still be caught by scope validation in Development.
Can you resolve a keyed service without constructor injection?

Yes - IServiceProvider has GetRequiredKeyedService<T>(key). I would use this in factory code or Program.cs setup where no constructor is available. Inside business logic I avoid it for the same reason as the regular service locator - it hides dependencies.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the DI container in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: The DI container is the built-in IoC container exposed as IServiceProvider. It holds all service registrations and is responsible for creating, injecting, and disposing service instances according to their configured lifetimes.
answer.detail: When you call builder.Build(), ASP.NET Core compiles all the registrations in IServiceCollection into an IServiceProvider. This provider is the container that lives for the entire application lifetime. When an HTTP request arrives, the framework creates a child IServiceProvider scoped to that request for resolving Scoped services. The container reads constructor parameters using reflection and resolves each dependency recursively - if ShipmentService needs IOrderRepository, and IOrderRepository needs AppDbContext, the container builds all three in the correct order. ASP.NET Core's built-in container is intentionally minimal - it does not support decorators, property injection, or convention-based scanning. For those features you can swap in a third-party container like Autofac by implementing IServiceProviderFactory.
keyPoints: IServiceProvider is the runtime interface to the container - it creates and resolves services by type.
IServiceCollection is just a list of registrations before Build() - it is not the container itself.
The container resolves dependency graphs recursively using reflection to read constructor parameters.
A child scope is created per HTTP request to isolate Scoped services from each other.
The built-in container is minimal by design - swap in Autofac when you need decorators or convention-based assembly scanning.

code.note: IServiceCollection holds registrations only. After Build(), IServiceProvider (via app.Services) is what you use to resolve instances.

answer.analogy: IServiceCollection is the recipe book; IServiceProvider is the chef who reads it and makes the dish.
redFlags: Confusing IServiceCollection with IServiceProvider - one is the registration list, the other is the runtime resolver.
Not knowing the container is built and locked when Build() is called.
Saying the built-in container supports decorators - it does not, which is one reason Autofac exists.
Not knowing that a child scope is created per HTTP request to isolate Scoped services.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between IServiceCollection and IServiceProvider?
followUpsQA.a: IServiceCollection is the list of registrations you build up before Build() is called. IServiceProvider is the compiled container created by Build() that actually resolves and creates service instances at runtime.

followUpsQA.q: When is the container built and locked?
followUpsQA.a: When you call builder.Build(). After that the service collection is frozen and new registrations cannot be added.

followUpsQA.q: Why might you replace the built-in container with Autofac?
followUpsQA.a: The built-in container does not support decorators, property injection, or convention-based assembly scanning. Autofac provides all of these, which are valuable in large codebases with many services.

followUpsQA.q: How does the container resolve a graph of dependencies?
followUpsQA.a: It uses reflection to read the constructor parameters of the requested type, then recursively resolves each parameter from its registrations, creating the full object graph bottom-up in the correct order.

dialogue.text: What is the DI container in ASP.NET Core and how does it work internally?

The DI container is exposed as IServiceProvider. You register services into IServiceCollection before calling builder.Build(). At that point ASP.NET Core compiles those registrations into an IServiceProvider, which is what resolves and creates instances at runtime for the rest of the app's life.
What is the difference between IServiceCollection and IServiceProvider?

IServiceCollection is just a list of descriptors - the recipe book. IServiceProvider is the compiled container - the chef who reads the book and makes the dish. You build up the collection before Build() and then only use the provider after Build() returns.
How does the container actually resolve something like ShipmentService that depends on three other services?

It uses reflection to inspect ShipmentService's constructor parameters. For each parameter it recursively resolves the type from its registrations - so it might resolve IOrderRepository which itself needs AppDbContext, and so on. The result is the full dependency graph built bottom-up automatically.
When would the built-in container not be enough?

When I need decorators, property injection, or convention-based scanning - like 'register all classes in this assembly that implement IRepository'. The built-in container does not support these. I would plug in Autofac via UseServiceProviderFactory(new AutofacServiceProviderFactory()) in Program.cs.
How does the container handle Scoped services per request?

The framework creates a child IServiceProvider scope at the start of each HTTP request. Scoped services are resolved from that child scope and cached within it - every class within the same request gets the same instance. When the request ends, the scope is disposed and calls Dispose on all Scoped IDisposable services.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
